title: Conductometric Titration of Strong Acid vs Strong Base
aim: To determine the strength of a strong acid (HCl) by titrating it against a strong base (NaOH) using conductometry.
theory: The titration of a strong acid (e.g., HCl) with a strong base (e.g., NaOH) follows the neutralization reaction: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O. Since HCl and NaOH are strong electrolytes, they dissociate completely in water. As NaOH is added to HCl, the highly mobile H+ ions are replaced by less mobile Na+ ions, causing a decrease in conductivity. After reaching the equivalence point, the addition of excess NaOH leads to an increase in conductivity due to the addition of Na+ and OH- ions.
procedure: Pipette out 20 mL of HCl into a 250 mL beaker.
Add enough distilled water to submerge the conductivity probe.
Fill the burette with 0.1N NaOH solution.
Place the beaker on a magnetic stirrer and insert the stirring bar.
Place the conductivity probe into the beaker and start stirring gently.
Add 0.5 mL of NaOH from the burette and record the conductivity.
Continue adding NaOH in 0.5 mL increments and recording conductivity until the values show a significant increase.
Plot a graph of conductivity versus volume of NaOH added.

requiredFormula.description: Where N1 = Normality of Acid, V1 = Volume of Acid, N2 = Normality of Base, V2 = Volume of Base (at equivalence point).

safety: Handle HCl and NaOH with care as they are corrosive.
Wear safety goggles and lab coat.
In case of skin contact, wash immediately with plenty of water.
Ensure the conductivity meter is calibrated before use.
